mnemonics.title: Unix Philosophy Mnemonic
mnemonics.content: K.I.S.S. - Keep It Simple, Stupid - Small tools, one purpose each, combine for complex tasks

mnemonics.title: Kernel Responsibilities
mnemonics.content: D.E.P.S - Device management, Execution control, Process scheduling, Storage (file system)

mnemonics.title: File Permission Order
mnemonics.content: U.G.O - User (owner), Group, Others - always in this order when setting permissions

acronyms.usage: Originally called UNICS, emphasizing its capability to handle multiple users
Standards ensuring software portability across Unix-like systems
Text-based interface to interact with the operating system through commands
Visual interface with windows, icons, menus, and pointers
Unique number assigned to each process running in Unix

visualTips.visualization: Draw three concentric circles: innermost = Kernel (core), middle = Shell (interface), outermost = User Applications. Show arrows from outer to inner representing system calls.
Draw an inverted tree with root (/) at top, branches: /bin (binaries), /usr (user programs), /home (user files), /etc (config), /dev (devices). Each branch can have sub-branches.
Draw init (PID 1) at top, with child processes branching downward. Show parent-child relationships with lines connecting them, representing the process hierarchy.

analogies.analogy: The Kernel is like the engine of a car (does the real work), while the Shell is like the steering wheel and dashboard (interface for the driver to control the engine).
System calls are like the windows in a house - the only way for the inside (user programs) to communicate with the outside (kernel/hardware).
A file descriptor is like a seat number in a theater - it is a small integer that identifies a specific 'seat' (open file) that a process has reserved.
Like LEGO blocks - each small piece (tool) does one thing, but combining them creates infinite possibilities.
